data.title: Sudan: Two Years of Armed Conflict and the World’s First Famine in Seven Years
data.description:
Published by Action Against Hunger.

NORTHAMPTON, MA / ACCESS Newswire / April 15, 2025 / Today marks two years since the beginning of the armed conflict in Sudan, which has escalated into one of the world's largest humanitarian crises, with 30.4 million people in need of humanitarian assistance. The Famine Review Committee of the IPC system evaluated in August 2024 that Zamzam camp in North Darfur reached the famine threshold, making it the first formal declaration of famine in more than seven years. Now, 26 million people are facing acute levels of food insecurity in Sudan.

An estimated 12.1 million women and girls in Sudan need support against gender-based and sexual violence, but access to medical and psychosocial care is limited due to the ongoing conflict. Women and girls are especially likely to be displaced from their homes to escape threats of such violence and, in 2024, they represented over 50% of Sudan's refugees.

Conflict-related challenges have inhibited humanitarian organizations' ability to reach populations in need. "It has become very difficult to work in Sudan. We are worse off than yesterday, but we will continue to work to make the situation better," says Action Against Hunger's regional head for Africa, Paloma Martin de Miguel. Action Against Hunger reminds all parties to the conflict that prevention of sufficient food aid delivery, including attacks on food production and distribution infrastructure, are in direct violation of UN Security Council Resolution 2417, which should be respected.

Action Against Hunger has been present in Sudan since 2018 and has scaled up efforts to address this unprecedented humanitarian crisis. From April 2023 to December 2024, Action Against Hunger's teams supported 816,943 people with health and nutrition supplies in 15 locations across Blue Nile, Central Darfur, Red Sea, South Kordofan and White Nile. Interventions such as protection measures and medical and psychosocial support were provided to 11,953 people affected by gender-based violence.

Action Against Hunger urges the following actions to be taken immediately

  • That all parties to the conflict take urgent action to stem the humanitarian crisis in Sudan by refraining from attacking, looting or damaging infrastructure essential to the safety of the population such as markets, agricultural or livestock lands, and hospitals.

  • That there be accountability for human rights violations, particularly the reported incidents of gender and sexual based violence committed against women and girls, and that protection measures be strengthened to prevent further atrocities.

  • That the international community and humanitarian actors mobilize urgent aid, as the situation is critical and requires an immediate response.

***

Action Against Hunger leads the global movement to end hunger. We innovate solutions, advocate for change, and reach 21 million people every year with proven hunger prevention and treatment programs. As a nonprofit that works across 59 countries, our 8,900 dedicated staff members partner with communities to address the root causes of hunger, including climate change, conflict, inequity, and emergencies. We strive to create a world free from hunger, for everyone, for good.

View additional multimedia and more ESG storytelling from Action Against Hunger on 3blmedia.com.

Contact Info:

Spokesperson: Action Against Hunger

Website: https://www.3blmedia.com/profiles/action-against-hunger

Email: info@3blmedia.com

SOURCE: Action Against Hunger

View the original press release on ACCESS Newswire

THE LAWSUIT: A class action securities lawsuit was filed against Actinium Pharmaceuticals, Inc. that seeks to recover losses of shareholders who were adversely affected by alleged securities fraud between October 31, 2022 and August 2, 2024.

CASE DETAILS: The filed complaint alleges that defendants made false statements and/or concealed that: (1) the Company's data from the Phase 3 Sierra

trial was unlikely to satisfy the FDA's guidelines for the acceptance and approval of the Company's targeted radiotherapy, Iomab-B BLA; (2) the additional analyses, including long-term follow-ups that purportedly demonstrated a trend towards improved Overall Survival that the Company provided to the FDA in an attempt to mitigate the Sierra Trial's poor OS data were unlikely to satisfy the FDA's guidelines for the acceptance and approval of the Company's Iomab-B BLA; (3) as a result, the FDA would likely refuse to review the Iomab-B BLA or, if it did consider that BLA, that the application in its current form was unlikely to be approved; and (4), as a result, defendants' positive statements about the Company's business, operations, and prospects were materially misleading and/or lacked a reasonable basis.

data.title: Capital Power Upsizes Bought-Deal Share Offering to $450 Million
data.description:
Capital Power on Tuesday increased the size of its previously announced bought-deal offering for proceeds of about $450 million from an earlier target of about $350 million.

A group of underwriters agreed to buy, on a bought deal basis, 10.4-million shares of the company at $43.45 apiece for total proceeds of about $450 million.

The underwriters have also been granted an option to buy up to an additional 1.6-million shares at the offering price. If the over-allotment option is exercised in full, total proceeds from the public offering will be about $517 million.

Proceeds of the offering, and the private placement announced on Monday, will be used to fund a portion of the US$2.2-billion purchase price for the acquisition the of Hummel Station and Rolling Hills Generating in the U.S. Northeast.

Shares of the company were last seen down $0.865 at $45.335 on the Toronto Stock Exchange.

data.title: Hesai Group's AT128 Lidar Sensor to be Incorporated in Didi, GAC Aion's L4 Robotaxi
data.description:
Hesai Group said Tuesday its AT128 light detection and ranging, or lidar, sensor will be used in the first L4 production-ready Robotaxi vehicle.

Robotaxi is a collaboration between Didi Autonomous Driving and GAC Aion New Energy Automobile, and the L4 Robotaxi model is expected to enter mass production this year, according to the statement. The vehicle is set for initial deployment in 2026, said Hesai.

Shares of Hesai Group were down over 8% in recent Tuesday trading.

data.description:

THE LAWSUIT: A class action securities lawsuit was filed against Viatris Inc. that seeks to recover losses of shareholders who were adversely affected by alleged securities fraud between August 8, 2024 and February 26, 2025.

CASE DETAILS: According to the complaint, defendants provided investors with material information concerning the failed inspection of Viatris' Indore, India facility. Defendants' statements misrepresented the FDA's issuance of a warning letter and its impact on Viatris' operations. Defendants routinely referred to the impact of the warning letter as a mere "minor headwind" for the Company.

On February 27, 2025, Viatris announced its financial results for the fourth quarter and full fiscal year 2024 and provided disappointing fiscal 2025 guidance. The Company attributed below-expectation guidance on "the expected financial impact from Indore facility warning letter and import alert." Following this news, the price of Viatris' common stock declined dramatically. From a closing market price of $11.24 per share on February 26, 2025, Viatris' stock price fell to $9.53 per share on February 27, 2025, a decline of about 15.21% in the span of just a single day.

data.title: Market Chatter: Banks to Fund $2.2 Billion of Debt for ABC Tech's TI Fluid Acquisition
data.description:
A group of banks led by JPMorgan Chase and Citigroup will use their own balance sheets to fund about $2.2 billion in debt in support of ABC Technologies' acquisition of TI Fluid Systems, Bloomberg reported Tuesday, citing people familiar with the matter.

ABC, which is backed by Apollo Global Management , closed the deal for the auto supplier for an enterprise value of over $2.4 billion on Tuesday, Bloomberg reported, citing a statement.

JPMorgan, Citigroup and Apollo did not immediately reply to requests for comment from MT Newswires.

Disclaimer

Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.

data.title: Nigeria plans petroleum products stockpile to counter global supply shocks
data.description:
Nigeria plans to establish a national strategic petroleum products stockpile this year to safeguard its economy against disruptions in the international market, the petroleum products regulator said at a press briefing on Tuesday.

Farouk Ahmed, head of the Nigerian Midstream and Downstream Petroleum Regulatory Authority, said the reserve, which the country's oil law mandates, would mitigate supply shocks and enhance the nation's energy security.

Nigeria, despite its oil wealth, frequently experiences fuel shortages and long queues. The country aims to use expanding domestic refining capacity, particularly the 650,000-barrel-per-day Dangote Refinery, to build resilience against global supply fluctuations.

While Nigeria currently maintains petroleum products reserves to cover approximately 30 days of supply, Ahmed said the new National Strategic Stock, modelled on the United States' Strategic Petroleum Reserve, would be significantly larger.

He did not specify the volume of the planned reserves.

Nigeria's Petroleum Industry Law mandates the regulator to issue a bulk petroleum liquids storage licence to private depots that can hold products for as long as needed.

Startup of the Dangote Refinery in September, along with five smaller refineries, has significantly reduced Nigeria's gasoline imports from 50.8 million litres per day in September to 28.7 million litres per day last month.

Data from the regulator indicate that currently operational local refineries are projected to process 770,500 bpd until June.

The regulator expressed optimism that refining expansion could eventually eliminate the need for gasoline imports.

data.title: Littelfuse to Release First Quarter Financial Results After Market Close on April 29
data.description:
CHICAGO--(BUSINESS WIRE)--April 15, 2025--

Littelfuse, Inc. , a diversified, industrial technology manufacturing company empowering a sustainable, connected, and safer world, announced today that it will release financial results for its first quarter of fiscal 2025 after market close on Tuesday, April 29, 2025.

The press release and slide presentation will be available in the Investor Relations section of the company's website, Littelfuse.com. The company will host a conference call on Wednesday, April 30, 2025, at 9:00 a.m. Central Time. The conference call will be available via webcast from Littelfuse.com and available for replay on the company's website.

About Littelfuse

Littelfuse, Inc. is a diversified, industrial technology manufacturing company empowering a sustainable, connected, and safer world. Across more than 20 countries, and with approximately 16,000 global associates, we partner with customers to design and deliver innovative, reliable solutions. Serving over 100,000 end customers, our products are found in a variety of industrial, transportation, and electronics end markets--everywhere, every day. Learn more at Littelfuse.com.

data.title: Canadian E&P Companies Unlikely to Report Guidance Changes in Q1, RBC Capital Markets Says
data.description:
Canadian oil and gas exploration and production (E&P) companies are unlikely to report any material capex cuts or guidance changes through the first quarter, RBC Capital Markets said in a Monday note.

But RBC expects companies to flag guidance changes later in the year if the strip plays out.

According to RBC, its cash flow per share estimates are up 6% quarter over quarter while its estimated 2025 free cash flow yields now map to 8% (strip).

2025 capex/production currently points to +9%/+12% year over year in aggregate, RBC said.

RBC said its top names include ARC Resources and PrairieSky Royalty .

data.title: BBB National Programs Launches 'Beyond the Influence' Podcast to Promote Transparent and Authentic Influencer Advertising
data.description:
Today, BBB National Programs launches its podcast, "Beyond the Influence," a new series that delves into the world of influencer marketing, exploring the lives, partnerships, and best practices of renowned influencers and athletes.

New York, NY, April 15, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Today, BBB National Programs launches its podcast, "Beyond the Influence," a new series that delves into the world of influencer marketing, exploring the lives, partnerships, and best practices of renowned influencers and athletes.

Through candid conversations and expert insights, the series reveals the strategies behind successful influencer marketing and endorsement campaigns, emphasizing the importance of transparency and authenticity in partnerships.

Hosted by BBB National Programs’ Tueré Rodriguez, the podcast kicks off with guest Martin Bowier, Director of Influencer & Talent Marketing for the National Football League (NFL), who shares behind-the-scenes stories that provide listeners with an understanding of influencer marketing within one of the world's most popular sports leagues.

"This podcast is a valuable resource for influencers and brands, offering insights and practical advice on truthful and transparent advertising practices that drive responsible marketing campaigns," said Rodriguez. "These are authentic stories from the people who have lived them, providing listeners with real-world experiences and lessons."

Episode Guide

  • Episode 1: Tackling Influencer MarketingGuest: Martin Bowier, Director of Influencer & Talent Marketing, NFL
  • Episode 2: Beyond the Game: Clinton Portis on Perseverance, Brand Partnerships, and the Future of NILGuest: Clinton Portis, 2x Pro Bowl Running Back & Current Coach for Delaware State University Hornets
  • Episode 3: Beyond the Court: Ty Young's JourneyGuest: Tamera “Ty” Young, Former WNBA player & Current Assistant Coach for the Chicago Sky
  • Episode 4: Content Queens: Building a Personal BrandGuests: Olivia Davis and Zainab Okeowo, Fashion and Beauty Content Creators and Brand Ambassadors
  • Episode 5: Tackling Two Passions: Endurance on and off the CourtGuest: Tommie Harris, 3x Pro Bowl Defensive Tackle and Current Country Music Artist
  • Episode 6: Luxury, Lifestyle, and InfluenceGuests: Kirstie Pike and Christine Diaz, Award-Winning Luxury, Lifestyle, and Fashion Content Creators

Watch the trailer and subscribe to the podcast here.

About BBB National Programs: BBB National Programs, a non-profit organization, is the home of U.S. independent industry self-regulation, currently operating more than a dozen globally recognized programs that have been helping enhance consumer trust in business for more than 50 years. These programs provide third-party accountability and dispute resolution services that address existing and emerging industry issues, create a fairer playing field for businesses, and a better experience for consumers. BBB National Programs continues to evolve its work and grow its impact by providing business guidance and fostering best practices in arenas such as advertising, child-and-teen-directed marketing, data privacy, dispute resolution, automobile warranty, technology, and emerging areas. To learn more, visit bbbprograms.org.

data.title: Canada to Offer Tariff Relief for Auto, Factory SectorsUpdate
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA--Canada said Tuesday it is providing tariff relief to parts of the economy--most notably the automotive and manufacturing sectors--that need to import goods from the U.S. to make a final product.

Canada's Department of Finance said the move is aimed at providing companies some time to adjust their supply chains and become less dependent on U.S. suppliers. At a campaign stop in suburban Montreal, Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney said the relief provides automakers "a very, very strong incentive to keep production and jobs here in Canada."

Finance Minister François-Philippe Champagne said Canada-based automakers are eligible for relief from the payment of tariffs, or the refund of tariffs already paid, on U.S. imports. In response to President Trump's trade policy, Canada has imposed hefty tariffs on about $43 billion of U.S. goods entering the country.

Champagne said under the arrangement, automakers based in Canada would be eligible to import a certain number of U.S.-assembled vehicles free of the counter tariffs that Ottawa has imposed.

The tariff relief, Champagne said, is contingent on automakers continuing to produce vehicles in Canada and on following through on planned investments in the country. A spokeswoman for Canada's Department of Finance couldn't immediately provide further details on how the remission program would work and what inputs would be covered; and which specific planned investments officials referred to in a statement explaining the measures.

Champagne added that there would be a six-month tariff exemption for goods imported by companies that are used in manufacturing, food processing, and in support of public health and public safety.

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

data.title: Canada's Housing Starts Pull Back in March, Notes TD
data.description:
Canadian housing starts came in at 214,200 annualized units in March, marking a 3% month-on-month decline from February's robust level, said TD.

The six-month moving average of starts dipped by 0.7% month over month to 235,300 units.

March's decline was concentrated in the multi-family sector, with urban starts down 4% month over month to 160,300 units. Meanwhile, urban single-detached starts inched higher by 1% to 43,000 units.

Urban starts were down in four of 10 provinces:

— Ontario was the largest drag on the overall total, as starts plunged 18,000 to 38,600 units. In contrast, starts jumped by 8,000 to 55,200 units in Quebec and rose by 1,600 to 29,200 units in British Columbia.

— Starts increased across the Prairies (+2, 400 to 67, 700 units) lifted by Saskatchewan and Manitoba. Meanwhile, starts declined in Alberta. Starts edged higher in the Atlantic (+140 to 12, 600 units), supported by gains in Nova Scotia and New Brunswick.

March's decline in housing starts surprised consensus expectations to the downside, stated the bank. However, it was consistent with TD's view that homebuilding will moderate amid past declines in demand and a sharp slowdown in population growth.

This view is centered on a pullback in housing starts in Ontario — where condo construction is cratering, pointed out the bank. March's data was a glaring example, given the steep drop in starts in Canada's largest province last month.

More broadly, Canadian homebuilding is likely to remain subdued moving forward amid economic uncertainty, weak population growth and elevated construction costs, added TD.

data.title: Motorsport Games Raises $2.5 Million in Private Placement
data.description:
Motorsport Games said Tuesday it raised $2.5 million in a private placement offering by issuing roughly 1.9 million class A common shares at $1.10 apiece.

The offering also involves a pre-funded warrant to buy up to 377,836 class A common shares at $1.0999 apiece, according to the company.

The strategic investment, led by a Pimax Innovation affiliate as well as institutional and accredited investors, closed with gross proceeds of $2.4 million on April 11, the company said.

The closing of the remaining $100,000 is expected on April 15, it added.

The proceeds are expected to enhance the company's cash position, speed up product development, help regain compliance with Nasdaq listing standards, and fund general corporate expenses, according to a statement.

data.title: IEA Cuts Oil Demand Forecast as U.S. Tariffs Weigh on Economic GrowthCommodities Roundup
data.description:
MARKET MOVEMENTS:

  • Brent crude oil is down 0.3% to $64.71 a barrel.
  • European benchmark gas is flat at EUR34.44 a megawatt-hour.
  • Gold futures are up 0.4% to $3,237.90 a troy ounce.
  • LME three-month copper futures are down 0.5% at $9,170 a metric ton.

TOP STORY:

IEA Cuts Oil Demand Forecast as U.S. Tariffs Weigh on Economic Growth

Oil demand growth is forecast to be weaker than previously projected as escalating global trade tensions sparked by U.S. President Trump's tariffs weigh on the global economy, the International Energy Agency said.

"With arduous trade negotiations expected to take place during the coming 90-day reprieve on tariffs and possibly beyond, oil markets are in for a bumpy ride and considerable uncertainties hang over our forecasts for this year and next," according to the Paris-based agency.

The IEA revised the economic growth assumptions underpinning its oil demand estimates, cutting global GDP estimates to around 2.4% in 2025 and 2.5% in 2026, from 3.1% for both years previously.

As a result, global demand is now forecast to grow by 726,000 barrels a day this year from 1.03 million barrels a day previously, reaching an average of 103.5 million barrels a day. Next year, growth is expected to slow further to 692,000 barrels a day.

OTHER STORIES:

Tullow Oil to Sell Kenyan Assets to Gulf Energy for a Minimum of $120 Million

Tullow Oil said it agreed to sell its Kenyan assets to Gulf Energy for a minimum of $120 million in cash with the potential for additional royalty payments.

The oil-and-gas producer said Tuesday that it agreed terms to sell Tullow Kenya--which houses the entirety of its assets and interests in Kenya--to the Kenyan energy and infrastructure group for a near-term cash payment of $80 million with an additional payment due over several years.

--

TotalEnergies Expects First-Quarter Production Increase, Uptick in Refining Margins

TotalEnergies expects first-quarter hydrocarbon production to be at the high end of its quarterly guidance range and for earnings to be boosted by an on-quarter increase in refining margins.

The French energy major said Tuesday that it expects hydrocarbon production for the first three months of 2025 to be at the high end of its 2.5 million to 2.55 million barrels of oil-equivalent a day guidance range. This would be an increase on the 2.43 million oil-equivalent barrels a day it produced in the fourth quarter of last year, when it benefited from the ramp up of projects that had started earlier in the year.

MARKET TALKS:

USDA Reports New Flash Sale of Corn to Portugal — Market Talk

1011 ET - The USDA says that Portugal has purchased 110,000 metric tons of corn for delivery in the 2024/25 marketing year. The flash sale constitutes over half of the U.S. corn bought by the country last year, according to USDA data. Portugal has bought U.S. corn at an accelerated pace this marketing year, purchasing 394,587 tons from September through the first week of April. Most-active CBOT corn is flat, while soybeans fall 0.9% and wheat also slides 0.9%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

--

U.S. Natural Gas Extends Losses in Seasonal Downturn — Market Talk

1004 ET - U.S. natural gas futures are lower following three straight sessions of losses on weakening seasonal demand and lingering effects from broader market reaction to U.S. tariff battles. "Weather models, as they normally are in April, are bearish and LNG feedgas has contracted from near record highs," BOK Financial's Dennis Kissler says in a note. "We are in a shoulder month of rebuilding storage and market direction remains pressured to the downside." Nymex natural gas is off 1.6% at $3.272/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

--

Corn Up Early After USDA Reports Slower Planting — Market Talk

1001 ET - Slower-than-expected rate of corn planted in the U.S. in the past week provides futures with support in early trading. The USDA's weekly Crop Progress report, issued Monday, shows that just 4% of the corn crop has been planted so far this year. That's one point lower than the average pace of the past 5 years, and two points below this time last year. Most of the main corn-planting states, like Iowa or Nebraska, are only 1%-2% complete so far — with the bulk of the progress coming from Texas at 63% and Kansas at 11%. The planting season for crops in Texas begins earlier than in other regions, with Texas farmers often planting in February and March. CBOT corn rises 0.2% early. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

--

Soybeans Sink On Report Of China Halting Boeing Purchases — Market Talk

0955 ET - Soybean futures are leading the grains complex lower, on news that China has directed airlines in the country not to place new orders for Boeing jets, according to Bloomberg News. For soybean futures, the sign of China ramping up the stakes in its trade war with the U.S. is a main pressure point. "The U.S./China trade war appears to be intensifying again," says Doug Bergman of RCM Alternatives in a note. Soybeans fall 0.7%, while corn is up 0.1% and wheat sinks 0.6%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

--

Lower Demand Outlooks Keep Lid on Oil Prices — Market Talk

0942 ET - Crude futures are lower after the IEA cuts its 2025 demand growth estimate by around 300,000 barrels a day, citing an expected negative impact of U.S. tariffs on global economic growth. The downgrade to 726,000 b/d follows forecast reductions by OPEC and the U.S. EIA. The lower demand outlooks coincide with OPEC+ gradually unwinding production cuts, including plans to bring back 411,000 barrels a day in May. The IEA also reduced its 2025 supply growth estimate by 260,000 b/d to 1.2 million b/d on lower expected U.S. and Venezuelan output, and says the OPEC+ May increase could be a lot smaller given overproduction by some members. WTI is off 0.3% at $61.36 a barrel, and Brent is off 0.3% at $64.70 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

--

Gold Futures Rise on Continued Tariff Uncertainty, ECB Outlook — Market Talk

1348 GMT - Gold futures tick higher on tariff uncertainty and short-term monetary policy outlook. Futures are up 0.2% at $3,233.60 a troy ounce, near Friday's record high of $3,263/oz. The exemption of some electronics products from so-called reciprocal U.S. tariffs provided some relief to the stock market, though this was quickly reversed by the threat the products could be subject to sectoral tariffs, Commerzbank analysts say in a note. The uncertainty both boosted gold's safe-haven demand and weakened the U.S. dollar as an alternative safe asset, further enhancing gold's appeal, Commerzbank says. The European Central Bank is generally expected to cut interest rates this week and it will likely consider further easing given U.S. tariff uncertainty, a boon for noninterest bearing bullion, analysts add. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

--

Base Metal Prices Mixed; Chinese Copper Demand Looks Muted — Market Talk

1344 GMT - Base metal prices are mixed, with LME three-month copper down 1.1% at $9,121 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum flat at $2,377 a ton. Indicators for Chinese copper demand are subdued, with March imports higher on-month but 1.4% lower on-year, Commerzbank analysts say in a note. First-quarter imports overall were down 5.2% on-year, the main reason likely being the spike in market prices on tariff concerns, Commerzbank says. Copper prices had risen a near six-month peak in mid-March of $10,046.50 a ton, before sharply sliding to as low as $8,105 a ton in the days following President Trump's so-called "Liberation Day" tariffs announcement. The levies were worse than the market expected, driving down expectations of global growth and therefore industrial metal demand. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

--

Gold Futures Gain on Central Bank Buying, Investor Demand — Market Talk

1050 GMT - Gold futures rise close to all-time records on strong safe-haven demand. Futures are up 0.4% at $3,238.40 a troy ounce, close to Friday's record high of $3,263/oz. The precious metal has also gained on reports of the Czech central bank further boosting holdings, alongside the Chinese central bank's higher gold import quotas for commercial banks. With central banks keeping up gold purchases, a continuation of the support seen throughout 2024, the precious metal's next leg higher is being supported by incremental purchases from the investment community, SP Angel analysts say in a note. ETF holdings have climbed again to 89.1 million ounces from 88.7 million ounces on Monday, though this remains well below highs in 2020 and 2022, SP Angel says. As such, ETF demand has room to grow further. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

--

European Gas Price Rises as Market Remains Tight — Market Talk

1020 GMT - European natural-gas prices rise in midday trade, with the benchmark Dutch TTF contract up 2.7% at 35.42 euros a megawatt hour. "For now, fundamentals in the European gas market remain supportive," ING analysts say. The bloc ended the 2024-25 heating season with storage levels a little under 34% full, below the five-year average of 45%. "The region will need to see the strongest net injections since 2022. We may need to see more flexibility on these targets, something that member countries are discussing." Prices have been hit by broader risk-off moves in the market last week, and are still around 16% lower on the month. However, analysts say the market is still set to be tight throughout the year and any downward revision to forecast will require a tangible demand hit. ING currently forecasts TTF at 44 euros a megawatt hour. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

TotalEnergies' Update Leads to Lower Earnings Expectations — Market Talk 1019 GMT - TotalEnergies should deliver first-quarter adjusted net income of $4.27 billion, RBC Capital Markets analysts Biraj Borkhataria and Adnan Dhanani write. Ahead of the French energy major's update, the analysts had expected $4.67 billion. Earnings in its upstream division are still seen at $2.4 billion but its integrated LNG division should report $1.37 billion, down from a prior forecast of $1.53 billion, they write. Refining and chemicals' projected earnings are now $329 million from $423 million, while integrated power is forecast to deliver $488 million from earlier expectations of $637 million, they write. Shares rise 1% to 50.75 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

--

Palm Oil Ends Lower, Tracking Soybean Oil's Weakness — Market Talk

1014 GMT - Palm oil prices ended lower, tracking soybean oil's weakness overnight on the Chicago Board of Trade. Market volatility across global financial assets amid the trade tensions has weighed on the commodities market and added layers of uncertainty to the near-term direction of crude palm oil futures, Phillip Nova analyst Lim Tai An says in a note. Meanwhile, Malaysia's palm oil exports for April 1-15 are estimated to have risen 13.55% on month to 450,657 metric tons, according to cargo surveyor AmSpec Agri Malaysia. The Bursa Malaysia derivatives contract for June delivery fell 63 ringgit to 4,107 ringgit a ton. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

--

Base Metal Prices Mixed; JPM Cuts Demand Expectations on Tariffs — Market Talk

0926 GMT - Base metal prices are mixed, with LME three-month copper down 0.5% at $9,173.50 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum up 0.2% at $2,383.50 a ton. Cuts to economic expectations due to tariff uncertainty and the increased probability of a recession in 2025 mean steep cuts to metal demand estimates, JPMorgan analysts say in a note. The U.S. bank cuts its global copper and aluminum demand forecasts by 1%, flipping balance expectations from a deficit to a modest surplus. Risks to demand estimates are still skewed to the downside, JPM says. Focus is now increasingly shifting to China's possible reactions as export demand is expected to fall, including potential fiscal stimulus. JPM is bearish on metals near-term, and estimates second-quarter average prices of $8,300 a ton and $2,200 a ton for copper and aluminum, respectively. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

--

Oil Broadly Stable After Cuts to Demand Outlook — Market Talk

0921 GMT - Oil prices are broadly steady after the IEA and OPEC lowered their oil-demand growth forecasts, saying escalating trade tensions are expected to weigh on the global economy. Brent crude and WTI are both down 0.1% at $64.81 and $61.46 a barrel, respectively, after last week's market turmoil pushed prices to a four-year low. The IEA sharply cut its demand estimates, while OPEC remains more optimistic, but both acknowledged sweeping U.S. tariffs have hurt consumption. "The uncertainty is generating significant headwinds for global growth and, as a result, oil demand," ING analysts say. Traders will now be closely watching the next phase in global trade policies following U.S. President Trump's latest tariff exemptions, as well as U.S.-Iran talks set to be held on Saturday. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

Gold Futures Near Record High as Investors Seek Safe Assets — Market Talk

0818 GMT - Gold futures rise to near record highs on persistent safe-haven demand amid trade tensions. Futures are up 0.6% at $3,245.80 a troy ounce, near Friday's record high of $3,263/oz. The precious metal has surged on market uncertainty around President Trump's tariffs, increasing recession risks and escalating trade tensions. There are media reports that the Chinese central bank has issued new gold import quotas to commercial banks amid the strong demand. The People's Bank of China usually restricts physical bullion imports. Meanwhile, gold inventories on U.S. commodities exchange Comex have started to decline after surging earlier in the year on tariff concerns. This likely reflects the precious metal's exemption from levies after all, ING analysts say in a note. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

--

Oil Prices Tick Up Though Demand Concerns Still Weigh — Market Talk

0803 GMT - Oil prices rise, with Brent crude and WTI both up 0.5% at $65.19 a barrel and $61.84 a barrel, respectively. Oil prices continue to recover from multi-year lows though Brent remains down 7.7% on month as the market assesses the effects of U.S. President Trump's tariffs on global demand, ANZ Research analysts say in a note. Oil did recoup some losses after the Trump administration announced a delay to some tariffs, though gains were short-lived and overshadowed by demand concerns amid rising trade tensions, ANZ writes. Traders are also keeping an eye on Iranian oil exports, after U.S. Treasury officials told Singapore's banks and shippers to expect increased pressure on Iran's oil industry and Chinese buyers of the country's crude, ANZ adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

--

Comex Gold Futures' Rally Likely Still Intact, Chart Shows — Market Talk

0657 GMT - Comex gold futures' rally is likely still intact, based on the daily chart, RHB Retail Research's Joseph Chai says in a research report. Despite the dip, the futures overnight close above $3,200/oz reaffirms sustained buying interest and signals that bullish momentum remains in play, the analyst says. The relative strength index continues to hover above the 50% threshold. Also, the commodity is trading comfortably above the 20-day and 50-day simple moving averages, reinforcing its prevailing uptrend, the analyst says. Barring a break below support at $3,000/oz, the precious metal is expected to rise toward $3,300/oz in the near term, the analyst adds. Spot gold is 0.5% higher at $3,225.88/oz. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

--

Lithium Bottom Likely in Rear View, But Long Road Ahead — Market Talk

0321 GMT - UBS trims another 10% off its 2026 price forecast for lithium-rich spodumene, now projecting an average of US$763 a metric ton for next year versus spot price of US$770. It also pares its long-term price forecast by 7% to US$1,300. The bank's Australian analysts say that while the beleaguered lithium market is probably past its nadir, the battery ingredient is headed for a gradual recovery. The analysts pare their stock-price target on lithium producer Pilbara Minerals to A$1.50 from A$2.00. They upgrade Pilbara Minerals to a neutral from sell but cite valuation grounds. Pilbara Minerals is down 1.8% at A$1.39. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

--

Iron Ore Rises on Back of Strong Steel Mill Activities — Market Talk

0253 GMT - Iron ore prices are higher in early Asian trade. Steel mills are actively operating during the peak season, and the consumption of iron ore continues to pick up, Baocheng Futures says in a note. The average daily molten iron output and the use of imported iron ore at sample steel mills both increased on month last week, the brokerage says in a note. However, iron ore prices will likely fluctuate going forward given ongoing tariff issues, it says. The most-traded iron ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is 0.2% higher at CNY707.5 a ton. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

Write to Barcelona Editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: FBN, MORNINGS WITH MARIA INTERVIEW WITH REP. TOM TIFFANY (R-WI)
data.description:
TRANSCRIPT

April 15, 2025

NEWS PROGRAM

REP. TOM TIFFANY (R-WI)

FBN, MORNINGS WITH MARIA INTERVIEW WITH REP. TOM TIFFANY (R-WI)

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

20 East Thomas Road, Suite 2200

Phoenix, AZ 85012

asc.info@viqsolutions.com

Copyright 2025. Provided under license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

All materials herein are protected by United States copyright law

and/or license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc., and may not be

reproduced, distributed, transmitted, displayed, published or

broadcast without the prior written permission of

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

You may not alter or remove any trademark, copyright or other

notice from copies of the content.

FBN, MORNINGS WITH MARIA INTERVIEW WITH REP. TOM TIFFANY (R-WI)

APRIL 15, 2025

SPEAKERS:

REP. TOM TIFFANY (R-WI)

MARIA BARTIROMO, FBN ANCHOR

MARIA BARTIROMO, FOX BUSINESS NETWORK ANCHOR: Joining me now with where lawmakers are is Wisconsin Congressman Tom Tiffany. Congressman, good to see you. Thank you so much for being here. Big victory before you all left for the Easter recess in passing the budget resolution. What happens when you come back?

REP. TOM TIFFANY (R-WI): Yeah. That was a good week last week, including passing that budget resolution. So, we have our staffs working busily, as we speak, to create this budget reconciliation bill, but now the heavy lifting starts. Now we start negotiating between the House and the Senate what we're going to put in there, and we absolutely have to make the tax cuts permanent. But, I think we also — the big debate last week, if you remember, Maria, was about how much we're going to reduce in spending. The Senate sent our resolution back to us saying only $4 billion. That's why some of us held out last Wednesday. We want to see greater spending cuts, and that's what needs to happen. We need to have greater spending savings for the American taxpayer.

BARTIROMO: So, in order to pay for the new ideas which President Trump brought to the table, which is no tax on tips and no tax on Social Security, the idea was raised to raise taxes on the rich, and I asked your colleague, Chairman of Freedom Caucus, Andy Harris, about it last week. Here he is. Watch.

(BEGIN VIDEO CLIP)

REP. ANDY HARRIS (R-MD): But, if we can't get enough spending reductions, we're going to have to pay for our tax cuts again to make sure that we don't go down the road of fiscal deterioration on this. That's one possibility.

BARTIROMO: Tell me about this 40 percent tax bracket.

HARRIS: Before the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, the highest bracket was 39.6 percent —

BARTIROMO: Yes.

HARRIS: — and it was less than a million dollars. Ideally, what we could do, again, if we can't find spending reductions, is say, OK, let's restore that higher bracket. Let's set it at maybe $2 million income and above to help pay for the rest of the President's agenda, which is, of course, no tax on tips and potentially other tax cuts.

(END VIDEO CLIP)

BARTIROMO: Where are you on that, congressman?

TIFFANY: I'm not a big fan of tax cut, or, excuse me, tax increases on anybody. But, I think the Department of Government Efficiency has identified plenty of savings. There is plenty of room to find the savings out there, as has been identified over the last couple of months, and that's part of the reason in the polling that you're showing there, Maria, it shows, what is it, almost two thirds of Americans are saying we can reduce spending. They've seen the news stories about DOGE and what they are doing. There is plenty of room to get those savings.

BARTIROMO: All right. And we also asked Mike Johnson, Speaker of the House, about it, and he was with me on Sunday. Here is his answer to that.

(BEGIN VIDEO CLIP)

BARTIROMO: The idea that you can have a new tax bracket of 40 percent for the highest earners, are you seriously considering that?

REP. MIKE JOHNSON (R-LA): Well, I'm not a big fan of doing that. I mean, we're the Republican Party and we're for tax reduction for everyone. So, I mean, that's a general principle that we always try to abide by. There is lots of discussion, lots of ideas on the Hill. People have different thoughts and theories on how we can find this perfect — solve this perfect equation to get all of this done, but I wouldn't put any money on any of that yet.

(END VIDEO CLIP)

BARTIROMO: All right. So, you are in alignment. It sounds like the White House — it sounds like the leadership in the House does not want that, but I want to get your take on where the offsets are then, because you're going to need full agreement here within the Republicans. You can't afford too many Nays. So, where do the cuts come from to pay for the tax cuts on the lowest earners?

TIFFANY: I think it's in a variety of programs, but it starts with waste, fraud and abuse. And you take some of the stuff that DOGE has identified. I mean, for example, The Wall Street Journal just reported a couple of weeks ago that there is $200 billion that was given out in duplicate payments in the Medicaid program. Americans don't want that type of waste going on. That's just one small example of ways in which we can do it. I also believe there should be work requirements, and work requirements will get us some savings in some of the welfare programs.

BARTIROMO: OK. Meanwhile, the White House is asking Congress to claw back more than $9 billion in previously approved funding. Some of this funding is incredible to me, congressman. Some of the rescinding requests include more than a billion dollars for NPR and PBS, a billion dollars that taxpayers give PBS and NPR, $6 million in for Net Zero cities in Mexico, $3 million for Iraqi Sesame Street, $1 million for voter ID in Haiti, $750,000 spent on reducing xenophobia in Venezuela, and $8,000 for promoting vegan food in Zambia. I'd love to know how all of this got passed in the first place.

TIFFANY: How about $5,600 for watering eight plants in the Department of Defense facility? I believe Senator Johnson from Wisconsin, my home state, has a very good idea. He says, let's go through this line by line. When he talks about going back to pre-pandemic spending, then increases for inflation, Social Security and Medicare, I think that's a really good approach, and we need to go through the budget line by line, and we can root that stuff out.

BARTIROMO: OK. Emily, jump in here.

EMILY DOMENECH, SVP, BOUNDARY STONE PARTNERS, & FMR MCCARTHY SR

POLICY ADVISOR: Congressman, I just want to follow up here, because obviously it's great to see a rescissions package coming from the White House, but at $9 billion, we are way shy of the numbers we need to really offset the extension of TCJA, and you say going through the budget line by line, but most of this isn't approved by Congress. It's discretion given away by Congress to the agencies. So, what are you going to do to try to claw back some of that control and make sure that Congress really can be accountable to every dollar that's spent?

TIFFANY: You go through the budget line by line, and you put those reductions in place, and I think you start with this stuff that's been identified by the Department of Government Efficiency and the White House, and you start to pull that back line by line. There is going to be tough votes, because Congress is not used to pulling money back that's already been authorized. It's time to do that, and we need to have the will to do it. Two thirds of the American public agrees with us that we need to reduce spending. And by the way, we are going to have to do it alone as Republicans, because Democrats have shown they don't care about fiscal responsibility. They've given us inflation over the last four years. We are in this on our own, but we need to live up to the mandate that was given to us on November 5th.

BARTIROMO: Well, congressman, you just introduced a resolution to withdraw the U.S. from the World Trade Organization over its handling of China. Tell us more, because I want to also get your take on some of these tariffs and the impact.

TIFFANY: Yes. So, every five years, we get the ability, as Congress, to review whether we should belong to the World Trade Organization. That five years is up now. I introduced a resolution saying, let's have that vote. It's time to find out if members of Congress stand with America, whether they stand with Wall — excuse me, Main Street, or do they stand with China and its allies. We've seen our manufacturing and farmers suffer over the last 20 years as a result of these trade agreements that have been struck and China steals our intellectual property. They use forced labor. It's time to look out for Americans. It's time to have a vote in Congress. Who do you stand with? The American people or the World Trade Organization?

BARTIROMO: Yeah. I agree. I mean, Corporate America has to start recognizing the national security threats here, and not just the economic impact of these tariffs. But, when you're talking about China retaliation, their tariffs are going to be stacking. They're talking about 125 percent baseline tariff on U.S. goods, likely stacking along with the retaliatory tariffs on agriculture, rare metal magnet and rare earth-related export controls. They've already said we're not going to send rare earth minerals to the United States. They also say we're not going to accept planes from Boeing, right? U.S. companies being put on the unreliable list and antitrust investigations. What do you make of China's retaliation toward America?

TIFFANY: I say, bring it on. It is time that we have this fight. Maria.

BARTIROMO: OK.

TIFFANY: We need to have the debate. We need to decide. Are we going to let China dictate to us as they have been?

BARTIROMO: Yeah.

TIFFANY: Look at the last four years under the previous administration. They did nothing to take on China. We have to do that. And by the way, in regards to the tariffs issue, we need to make sure that we have the work requirements I identified earlier, as well as the regulatory reform, because in order to build mills and factories, it can't take five to 10 years to get the permits to do it. The administration needs to —

BARTIROMO: Yeah.

TIFFANY: — really stay after this in regards to permitting reform so that we can build those mills and factories here. The American people are ready.

BARTIROMO: Yeah. I certainly think President Trump is making that a priority, permitting legislation or permitting executive orders, for sure. We got to see if Congress can codify those. But, you're right on China. I mean, from surveillance balloons to bullying our ships to buying land near military installations, it never stops.

Congressman, it's good to see you. Thank you, sir. Tom Tiffany joining us. We'll keep following your work.

TIFFANY: Happy Tax Day, Maria.

BARTIROMO: And to you.

END
